coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of multi-color fluorine and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots for use in tetracycline detection, colorful solid fluorescent ink, and film
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Changxing
Chen, Da
Yang, Yongsheng
Tang, Siyuan
Li, Xiameng
Xie, Feng
Wang, Gang
Guo, Qinglei
coredata.dc:description: Fluorine-doped graphene quantum dots have unique chemical bonds and charge distribution, which can bring unexpected properties compared to other common atom-doped graphene quantum dots. In the present work, fluorine and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (F, N-GQDs) are synthesized from levofloxacin via a simple hydrothermal method. Systematic studies demonstrate that F, N-GQDs can emit various fluorescence with the wavelength ranging from blue to green by dispersing F, N-GQDs into different solvents. Moreover, multi-color fluorescence is available by simply changing the concentration of F, N-GQDs. In addition to these unique characteristics, F, N-GQDs also exhibit a sensitive fluorescence response to tetracycline with an ultralow detection limit of 77 nM in water. Because of high photostability and high quantum yield, the F, N-GQDs are exploited as a unique invisible ink, which is printable and writable on paper. Meanwhile, based on the solvatochromism of F, N-GQDs, we realized the color adjustable fluorescent ink. Finally, large-area flexible multi-color fluorescent films are realized. Our synthesized F, N-GQDs, with tunable fluorescence in wavelength and intensity, have numerous opportunities for optical molecular sensors, information security, flexible optics, and others.
coredata.dcterms:subject: F, N-GQDs
Tunable fluorescence
Optical molecular sensor
Fluorescent ink
Flexible optics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Solvatochromism induced multicolor emission of F, N-GQDs
Concentration-dependent emission behavior of F, N-GQDs
A colorful fluorescent F, N-GQDs ink for patterning and writing
Multicolor film based on concentration effect of F, N-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of ASIC & System

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204 , 61604084 , and 51802337 ), the State Key Laboratory of ASIC & System (Grant No. 2020KF007), K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2017A610104). The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the F, N-GQDs. Inset: high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) and FFT images of F, N-GQDs. (b) Particle size distribution measured by TEM. (c) XRD pattern of F, N-GQDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of F, N-GQDs. (e) XPS spectrum of F, N-GQDs. (f-i) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (f), O 1s (g), N 1s (h) and F 1s (i).

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of F, N-GQDs. The insets show the optical images of F, N-GQDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and a 365 nm illumination (right). (b) Fluorescence spectra of F, N-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Excitation-emission matrix graph maps for F, N-GQDs. The fluorescence intensity of F, N-GQDs under (d) pH = 3–12, (e) ultraviolet irradiation for different timescales (150 W and 365 nm mercury lamp) and (f) different ionic strengths of NaCl aqueous solution.

(a) Normalized PL emission spectra of the F, N-GQDs in different solvents at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. (b) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of the F, N-GQDs in different solvents. (c) Emission wavelength as a function of the solvent polarity parameter, ET N; the solid line is a linear fit of the data. (d) Normalized PL spectra of the F, N-GQDs with different concentrations at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of the F, N-GQDs dispersion with various concentrations. (f) The plot of emission wavelength of F, N-GQDs with various concentrations. (g) Schematic illustration of the possible mechanism of F, N-GQDs with solvatochromism and concentration-dependent emission behaviors.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of F, N-GQDs with gradually increased concentration of TC in water. (b) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) and the concentration of TC. Inset: linear fitting between F/F0 and the concentration of TC within a small range from 2 to 30 μM. (c) Images of F, N-GQDs in the presence of various concentrations of TC under the excitation of a UV light and sunlight. (d) The UV–vis absorption spectra of F, N-GQDs, TC and the mixture of F, N-GQDs and TC. (e) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TC, and the PLE and PL spectra of the F, N-GQDs. (f) The fluorescence decay profile for the F, N-GQDs in the absence and presence of TC.

(a) Images of imprinted Chinese/English characters and 2D codes based on F, N-GQDs inks in different solvents (i.e., water, DMF and acetone; 0.2 mg/mL). Images of selectively printed logo of “Ningbo University” based on F, N-GQDs inks in different solvents under (b) the daylight and (c) UV light irradiation. Images of the fluorescence paper crane under (d) the daylight and (e) UV light irradiation. Images of hand-written sentences using the F, N-GQD-based fluorescence ink on a rice paper under (f) the daylight and (g) UV light irradiation.

(a) Fluorescent film preparation flow chart. (b) Photographs of fluorescent films with different F, N-GQDs content under 365 UV light irradiation (the content increases from left to right). (c) The normalized optimal fluorescence spectrum of films. (d) The UV absorption spectrum of films. (e) Tauc-Plot for bandgap calculation in films with different F, N-GQDs content.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of F, N-GQDs and their fluorescent images under different solvents and different concentrations.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multi-color fluorine and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots for use in tetracycline detection, colorful solid fluorescent ink, and film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Microelectronics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Microelectronics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of ASIC and Systems, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of ASIC and Systems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of ASIC and Systems, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Microelectronics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China (Q. Guo).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorine-doped graphene quantum dots have unique chemical bonds and charge distribution, which can bring unexpected properties compared to other common atom-doped graphene quantum dots. In the present work, fluorine and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (F, N-GQDs) are synthesized from levofloxacin via a simple hydrothermal method. Systematic studies demonstrate that F, N-GQDs can emit various fluorescence with the wavelength ranging from blue to green by dispersing F, N-GQDs into different solvents. Moreover, multi-color fluorescence is available by simply changing the concentration of F, N-GQDs. In addition to these unique characteristics, F, N-GQDs also exhibit a sensitive fluorescence response to tetracycline with an ultralow detection limit of 77 nM in water. Because of high photostability and high quantum yield, the F, N-GQDs are exploited as a unique invisible ink, which is printable and writable on paper. Meanwhile, based on the solvatochromism of F, N-GQDs, we realized the color adjustable fluorescent ink. Finally, large-area flexible multi-color fluorescent films are realized. Our synthesized F, N-GQDs, with tunable fluorescence in wavelength and intensity, have numerous opportunities for optical molecular sensors, information security, flexible optics, and others.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is becoming a rising star in the family of fluorescent materials. Compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs exhibit an excellent fluorescence brightness and superior photostability [1,2]. Moreover, rich functional groups existing on GQDs edges enable the modulation and control of their physical or chemical properties, such as fluorescence via optical excitation [3], bandgap for electronic transition [4], solubility in water solvents [5], electrochemical properties [6,7] and biological compatibility [8,9]. To tune the photoluminescence property and expand the application scope of GQDs, heteroatoms doping is a general and effective strategy [10–14]. To date, nitrogen, phosphorus and sulfur are commonly used atoms to dope GQDs [10,15,16]. However, there is still a requirement to explore new doping atoms to enhance the performance of GQDs. Fluorine (F) is one of the most common elements in the earth, and it is powerful in modulating the luminescent [17], electronic [18], electrochemical [19] and magnetic properties [20] of graphene owing to high electronegativity. However, there are few reports on fluorine-doped graphene quantum dots due to the big difference in electronegativity between fluorine and carbon atoms [21]. More importantly, when bonded with F to form C-F bonds, which allows for strong and highly polarizations, GQDs will have unique properties, such as reduced surface energy and enhanced dipole interaction [22,23].

In addition to searching novel doping elements to improve the performance of GQDs, the development of GQDs still faces several important challenges, for example the multi-color emission in GQDs. Most of the presented developed GQDs exhibit a single color emission, with a relatively low emission quantum yield, thereby limiting their applications [10,15]. Despite multi-color emissive GQDs could be synthesized by adjusting reactants and/or reaction condition, such multi-step process is relatively time-consuming with complex operation procedures [14,23]. Therefore, it is of great significance to seek more facile route, such as the introduction of solvent effect or concentration effect [24–26], to realize multi-color emission in GQDs. In addition, GQDs-based solid state fluorescence applications are not satisfactory due to strong luminescence quenching in the solid aggregate state [27]. Although previous research has focused on embedding GQDs in solid matrices or polymers to prevent their agglomeration, these matrices usually restrict the optical performance due to the light absorption. Until now, only few groups have reported on the investigation of the luminescence from GQDs in a solid state [28], and the multi-color fluorescence from GQDs in the solid state is rarely studied. Therefore, the development of a facile strategy for preparing multi-color emission based on GQDs is desirable.

The efficient, fast and reliable detection of antibiotics is of great significance for environmental safety and human health. Tetracycline (TC) is widely used as an antibiotics in veterinary and human medicine owing to its broad-spectrum activity for pathogenic microorganisms [29]. However, some side-effects can raise from the abuse of TCs, including liver damage, drug resistance and allergic reactions [30,31]. Traditional detection methods for TCs contain high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), high-performance capillary electrophoresis (HPCE) and liquid chromatograph-mass spectrometer (LC-MS) [32–34]. However, most of them involve sophisticated instruments and complex processes. Therefore, a simple, rapid and effective analytical method for the determination of TCs is greatly needed.

In this paper, we report a facile hydrothermal route to prepare fluorine and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (F, N-GQDs), which have tunable photoluminescence (PL) from blue to yellowish green via a synergy effect of solvatochromism and concentration engineering (Scheme 1 ). Systematical characterizations reveal that the as-prepared F, N-GQDs show different emission colors, which can be controlled by the polarity of solvents. With the increase of solvent polarity, the emission wavelength shifts from 425 to 502 nm. In addition, the synthesized F, N-GQDs also have a concentration-dependent PL emission behavior, with a tunable emission wavelength from 493 to 536 nm. In addition to these unique characteristics, F, N-GQDs also exhibit a sensitive fluorescence response to tetracycline with an ultralow detection limit of 77 nM in water. Because of solvatochromism and high quantum yield, the F, N-GQDs are exploited as a unique invisible ink with adjustable color, which is printable and writable on paper. Finally, large-area flexible multi-color fluorescent films are realized.

All chemical reagents are purchased as analytical grade without any further purification. Levofloxacin, ethanol, formamide, N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethyl acetate, ethyl ether, toluene and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) are purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Deionized water throughout all experiments is obtained from the Millipore system.

During the hydrothermal synthesis of green-emissive F, N-GQDs, 1 mmol of levofloxacin is dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water with ultrasonic treatment. Then the solution is transferred to a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 8 h. After that, the sample is naturally cooled to the room temperature. The obtained solution is purified via a 0.22 μm filter to remove large particles, and through dialysis with molecular weight cut off (MWCO) of 1000 Da. Finally, the purified solution is freeze-dried to obtain F, N-GQDs powder.

Morphologies of the F, N-GQDs are characterized by using transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi H-8100) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Oxford Instruments, Cypher S). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, PHI Quantera II system, Ulvac-PHI, INC, Japan) of F, N-GQDs are obtained with a PHI1580EXCA photoelectron spectrometer. A spectrometer (Bruker, WQF-410) is used to obtain Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of F, N-GQDs. Raman spectra (HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR800) are obtained using an Ar+ laser with the wavelength of 532 nm. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption properties of F, N-GQDs are characterized by a UV-5800 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra are collected by a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.) at room temperature. A SCIENTZ-100F freeze dryer is used to obtain F, N-GQDs solid powder.

The quantum yield of the F, N-GQDs is measured according to the previous report. Specially, rhodamine 6G (QY = 95% in ethanol, excitation wavelength 420 nm) is selected as the reference. A series of F, N-GQDs solutions with different concentrations are prepared to ensure all corresponding optical absorbance values are within 0–0.1. The QY of F, N-GQDs is then calculated according to the following equation:[35]
QY =
Q
ST
×
K X
K
ST
×
η X
η
ST
2

where η is the refractive index (1.33 for water and 1.36 for ethanol), K is the slope of integrated emission intensity against absorbance. The subscripts “ST” and “X” refer to the reference and target sample, respectively.

The detection of TC using F, N-GQDs is performed at room temperature in deionized water. F, N-GQDs is dispersed into 10 mmol/L phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer solution at pH 6.5–7.5. Subsequently, different concentration of 1.5 mL TC and 1.5 mL of F, N-GQDs solutions (0.064 mg/mL) are mixed into centrifuge tube. The fluorescence spectrum is recorded at room temperature under an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

The TEM image in Fig. 1
(a) reveals that F, N-GQDs are spherical and mono-dispersed. The size distribution of F, N-GQDs ranges from 2 and 6 nm, with an average diameter of 3.9 nm (Fig. 1 (b)). The inserted high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image clearly shows that the lattice spacing of F, N-GQDs is about 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite [36,37], which reveals that the F, N-GQDs contain graphite-like structures. The AFM result ( Figure S1
(a)) also supports the spherical morphology of F, N-GQDs with an average thickness of 0.89 nm. Structural properties of the as-synthesized F, N-GQDs are further analyzed by XRD and Raman scattering spectra. As shown in Fig. 1 (c), two broad humps at approximately 21° and 43° appear in the XRD result, therefore, the interlayer spacing is estimated to be 0.42 and 0.21 nm [24,35]. As shown in Figure S1
(b), the Raman result of F, N-GQDs presents two characteristic peaks at 1352 (disorder D bond) and 1583 cm−1 (graphite G bond). The ratio of G to D bands is 1.19, indicating the high crystallinity of the F, N-GQDs [38].

Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are employed to identify the surface functional groups and chemical elements of F, N-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 (d), the F, N-GQDs show the broad absorption bands from 3250 to 3482 cm−1, indicating a large amount of amino (NH) or hydroxyl (OH) groups existing on the surface [25]. The absorption peak between 2858 and 2969 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of CH bonds [25]. The vibrational absorption bands of CO, CC, CN and the epoxy group (COC) appear at around 1619, 1549, 1333 and 1044 cm−1 [16,39–41], respectively. More importantly, the obtained FT-IR spectrum exhibits significant absorption peaks of semi-ionic CF bonds at 1260 cm−1, covalent CF bonds at 1101 cm−1 [7,42]. The full XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 1 (e) shows four peaks at 285.5, 399.4, 532 and 686.8 eV, suggesting that the F, N-GQDs consist of C, N, O and F. Meanwhile the atomic concentration of C, N, O and F in F, N-GQDs ( Figure S1
(c)) are calculated to 70.38%, 12.99%, 14.01% and 2.62%, respectively. In Fig. 1 (f), the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s band can be divided into three peaks at 284.15, 285.75 and 287.17 eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, CN/CO and C-F, respectively [2,42–44]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 (g)) can be divided into three peaks at about 530.37, 532.96 and 535.57 eV, which confirms the presence of CO, CO and COC, respectively [25,45]. The N 1 s XPS spectrum of F, N-GQDs (Fig. 1 (h)) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.99, 400.21 and 401.33 eV, indicating the exist of pyridinic N, amino N and graphitic N [7,42,46]. The deconvoluted F 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 (i)) exhibits two different bonding configurations at 688.79 and 686.53 eV, which are assigned as the covalent CF bond and semi-ionic CF, respectively [7,42]. Specifically, the XPS spectrum analysis is well consistent with the FT-IR result, confirming that fluorine and nitrogen atoms have been successfully introduced into F, N-GQDs. Due to abundant hydrophilic groups like HN2, OH and CF on the surface, an uniform dispersion of F, N-GQDs in water is available [47,48].

The UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and PL emission of the as-prepared F, N-GQDs solution are measured at room temperature to evaluate their optical properties. As shown in Fig. 2
(a), the as-synthesized F, N-GQDs present five UV–vis absorption peaks located at 226, 253, 285, 335 and 350 nm. Three peaks at 226, 253 and 285 nm are categorized as π-π* transitions of CC bond [36,41,49,50], while the other two peaks around 335 and 350 nm are attributed to the n-π* transitions of CO bond [23,24,28]. Notably, F, N-GQDs exhibit an unusual weak broad absorption from 377 to 415 nm, which might be ascribed to the n-π* transition of surface states or the defect bonds (CN/C-F) [2,42]. Under the maximum excitation (λex = 390 nm), the F, N-GQDs aqueous solution exhibit bright green fluorescence with a maximum emission wavelength (λem) of 513 nm, and the diluted solution is nearly colorless under the daylight illumination. Moreover, the F, N-GQDs aqueous solution exhibits an excitation-dependent PL feature (Fig. 2 (b,c)). The quantum yield (QY) of the F, N-GQDs is calculated to be 78%. The fluorescence stability of F, N-GQDs in various conditions determines their practical applications. Therefore, the fluorescence stability of F, N-GQDs under different environments is investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 (d), our synthesized F, N-GQDs exhibit good stability at a wide pH range from 3 to 12. And the change in fluorescence intensity of F, N-GQDs is negligible at a 24-hour photobleaching condition, as shown in Fig. 2 (e). The long-term storage stability up to 24 days is also demonstrated, as shown in Figure S2 . In addition, the fluorescence of F, N-GQDs exhibits superior stability even when the concentration of NaCl is higher than 2 mol/L, as shown in Fig. 2 (f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvatochromism induced multicolor emission of F, N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The ability to tune the emission wavelength of fluorescent materials or even to enable a multicolor emission represents significant practical values for their future applications. Taking advantage of previous reports [25,51], PL emission spectra and UV–vis absorbance spectra of the F, N-GQDs dispersion in several solvents with different polarities are recorded. Fig. 3
(a) shows the normalized PL emission spectra of the F, N-GQDs dispersions, and an obvious red-shift from 425 nm (blue) to 502 nm (green) is found with the increase of the solvent polarity. The normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of each solution are shown in Fig. 3 (b). It can be clearly seen that the absorption peaks near 335 and 350 nm hardly change with the solvent polarity, while the broad absorption peaks at 377–415 nm have an obvious solvent dependence, indicating that the F, N-GQDs possess different surface states in different solvents. The detailed UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of each solvent are shown in the Figure S3 . Moreover, the relationship between PL excitation and PL emission is obtained by measuring the excitation-emission matrix graphs of F, N-GQDs in each solvent, and the results show the distinct excitation-dependent PL emission in all 9 solvents ( Figure S4 ). Table S1 summarizes the relative solvent polarity parameter (ET N) of the different solvents and the emission wavelengths of F, N-GQDs at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. A linear plot of the PL emission varying with the relative solvent polarity is fitted, as shown in Fig. 3 (c). These presented results clearly show that the F, N-GQDs have a solvatochromism behavior, thus inducing a solvent-dependent emission property.

Previous studies suggest that the origin of solvatochromism behavior could be due to the interaction between the solvent molecule and surface functional groups on the GQDs [25,51]. Thereby, this solvatochromism behavior in our F, N-GQDs can be attributed to the coexistence of surface functional groups and F, N co-doping. According to the XPS and FTIR results (Fig. 1 (d-i)), the as-synthesized F, N-GQDs contain nitrogen- and fluorine-groups, especially amine N, pyridinic N and C-F bonds. These functional groups have strong electronegativity, thus increasing the charge-carrier density due to their interaction with the surrounding solvents [51–55]. These interactions can generally be divided into hydrogen bond (HB) interactions and dipole–dipole interactions. The schematic mechanism for F, N-GQDs with a solvatochromism behavior is shown in Fig. 3 (g). For protic solvents (such as water, ethanol and formamide), the solvent-dependent emission behavior is dominated by the HB interaction between protic solvent and F, N-GQDs. Facilitated by HB, the amine N, pyridinic N and C-F bonds can attract more electrons from protic solvents, leading to a gradual decrease in the emission energy level of F, N-GQDs [51]. Notably, the polarity of ethanol is smaller than that of formamide, but the corresponding emission wavelength is much larger. For aprotic solvents without hydrogen bond donors, C-F bonds will dominate the intermolecular interaction. The introduction of highly polarized C-F bonds at the surface of F, N-GQDs results in relatively large amount of dipoles [12,22], and these dipoles can produce a strong dipole–dipole interaction (C-Fδ+•••Xδ+) with other close dipoles (solvent molecule) [52]. Therefore, a channel is formed to attract electrons, causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength of F, N-GQDs [51].

The presence of fluorine in the F, N-GQDs contributes to the exist of n-orbit, therefore, electrons are localized within this orbit rather than π-orbit due to the strong negative induction effect. The n-orbit will serve as the highest occupied molecular orbit (HOMO) and the PL should be originated from n-π* excitation. It is known that the negative induction effect can be affected by the polarity of the solvent, thereby, the excited electrons can be stabilized by adjusting the dipole moment direction of the surrounding molecules [12]. With the gradual increase of the solvent polarity, the negative induction effect strengthens and the ability to change the dipole direction of the surrounding molecules will also increase, which assist the energy level narrowing due to the electron transfer. As the solvent polarity becomes higher, the excited electrons lose more energy than those in the low polar solvent, thus resulting in the increase of the HOMO level and the red shift in the PL emission [55]. This hypothesis is supported by the red-shift of both absorption peak and emission peak at the solution with a high polarity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration-dependent emission behavior of F, N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the solvent effect, the synthesized F, N-GQDs also have a concentration-dependent PL emission behavior. As shown in normalized PL emission spectra of Fig. 3 (d), the emission wavelength of the synthesized F, N-GQDs shifts from 493 to 536 nm when the concentration varies from 0.02 to 0.2 mg/mL. UV–vis absorption spectra of the synthesized F, N-GQDs with different concentrations are shown in Fig. 3 (e). The absorption peak exhibits an obvious red shift with the increase of the concentration. Moreover, Fig. 3 (f) shows the emission wavelength varying as a function of the concentration, which is consistent with the UV–vis absorption spectra. The emission wavelengths of F, N-GQDs with several certain concentrations are shown in Table S2. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of F, N-GQDs is shown in Figure S5 , and the fluorescence intensity first increases with the increase of the concentration, then starts to decrease once the concentration reaches 0.064 mg/mL. The PL spectra of F, N-GQDs with different concentrations in Figure S6 reveal that all characterized samples have stable emission, which are dependent on the excitation wavelength.

As illustrated in Fig. 3 (g), the reason for the concentration-dependent luminescence property of F, N-GQDs is explained. It is known that the introduction of F atoms can reduce the surface energy and promote the interaction of interdot [23,44]. The presences of abundant amine N, pyridine nitrogen, hydroxyl and C-F surface groups on F, N-GQDs facilitate the formation of hydrogen bonds formation [47,51,56]. At a high concentration, the distance between adjacent F, N-GQDs is short, therefore the interaction among each other becomes strong, causing the self-assembly of aggregates. These aggregates usually have smaller band gap than that of a single GQD [57], resulting in the red-shift of the emission wavelength. Besides, the fluorescence intensity also exhibits a similar tendency to the emission wavelength as the concentration of F, N-GQDs varies. The increased PL intensity with concentration from 0.02 to 0.064 mg/mL induced the small aggregation of F, N-GQDs, which forms conjugated aromatic rings that oscillate with each other. Therefore, the energy of electrons in excited state is consumed, reducing the non-radiative decay of F, N-GQDs. However, as the concentration with further increases (from 0.064 to 0.2 mg/mL), more and more large aggregates are formed and molecules pack together tightly. Thus, the non-radiative relaxation among them is enhanced and the radiation channel is blocked, resulting in a much lower PL intensity [47].

The ability to selectively detect molecules for a GQD-based molecular sensor is important due to the complexity of a real environmental system. Many possible interfering metal ions, antibiotic and amino acid including Cd2+, K+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2 2+, Ag+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Al3+, tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), doxycycline (DC), l-Alanine, l-Serine, l-proline, l-aspartic, GSH are added into the F, N-GQDs aqueous solution (0.064 mg/mL) at a concentration of 0.1 mmol/L. As shown in Figure S7 , the fluorescence of F, N-GQDs are seriously quenched by TC, OTC, CTC and DC. It is worth noting that TC, OTC, CTC and DC are tetracyclines (TCs), and they have a similar structure. The result shows that the as-prepared F, N-GQDs have a strong selectivity for the detection of TCs. Furthermore, the PL emission spectra of F, N-GQDs aqueous solution in the presence of varying concentrations of TCs are recorded under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm (Fig. 4
(a)). As the concentration of TC increases, the emission intensity at 513 nm gradually decreases. Accordingly, as show in Fig. 4 (b), F/F0 decreases with the increase of TC concentration, and a good linear relationship in the range of 2–30 μM with a linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.989 (n = 7) is obtained, expressed as F/F0 = -0.0010CTC + 1.013, where the CTC is the concentration of TC, F and F0 represent the fluorescent intensities of F, N-GQDs with and without TC, respectively. Furthermore, the signal-to-noise ratio is 3 and the limit of detection is about 77 nM, which is comparable to other reported fluorescence detection probes (Table S3). In addition, the detection of TC in different solvent environments (ethanol, formamide and DMF) is also carried out. Detailed results are shown in the Figure S8 and Table S4. The detection limits of TC in alcohol, formamide and DMF are 68, 106 and 120 nM, respectively. There presented results demonstrate great potentials of F, N-GQDs for applications in environmental monitoring.

The fluorescence quenching phenomenon implies strong interaction between TC and F, N-GQDs. It is known that TC contains hydroxyl and carbonyl molecules, and the surface of F, N-GQDs has hydrophilic functional groups (NH2, CF, OH). Therefore, F, N-GQDs can form hydrogen bonds with TC to enhance the selectivity and detectivity [30]. To further understand the quenching mechanism of the fluorescence probe, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the F, N-GQDs aqueous solution added with TC is measured. As shown in Fig. 4 (d), compared with the absorption peak of pure F, N-GQDs solution, no new absorption peak appears. Besides, the FT-IR spectra of F, N-GQDs in the presence and absence of TC in Figure S9 show that no obvious shift and no new absorption site appear, indicating that no functional groups are formed or adhered at the surface of F, N-GQDs. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC and PL spectrum of F, N-GQDs are recorded in Fig. 4 (e). The broad absorption peak at 360 nm of TC has a large overlap with the excitation peak and a small overlap with the emission peak of F, N-GQDs. Similar UV–vis absorption spectrum is found in the other three solvents, as shown in Figure S10 . Therefore, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon is probably caused by either inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [30]. To seek the reason for the quenching phenomenon, the fluorescence lifetime of F, N-GQDs with and without TC is measured. As shown in Fig. 4 (f), the fluorescence lifetime of F, N-GQDs in the absence and presence of TC are 6.92 and 6.87 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime is almost constant, suggesting that the IFE dominates the fluorescence quenching [10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: A colorful fluorescent F, N-GQDs ink for patterning and writing
ce:section.ce:para:
The excellent stability, dispersibility and fluorescence make the F, N-GQDs become an alternative option for the fabrication of fluorescent inks. For the printing application, a series of F, N-GQDs solution is injected into a vacant seal, capable of printing characters, 2D code and the logo of Ningbo University. Benefiting from the unique solvatochromism behavior of F, N-GQDs, the multicolor fluorescent inks based on F, N-GQDs are formed by dispersing F, N-GQDs into different solvents (i.e., water, DMF and acetone; 0.2 mg/mL). Detailed printing process is shown in the Figure S11 . As shown in Fig. 5
(a), before the solvent completely evaporates, the imprinted Chinese/English characters and 2D codes show blue, cyan and green under UV light irradiation (365 nm). In addition, a multi-color logo of Ningbo University is obtained by selective print of these three different fluorescent inks on a rice paper, as shown in Fig. 5 (b-c). Moreover, the immersion of a rice paper in F, N-GQDs solution (0.02 mg/mL) will form a fluorescent paper after the full evaporation of solvent. Then the fluorescent paper is folded into a paper crane. As shown in the Fig. 5 (d-e), the paper crane shows the original color of the rice paper under sunlight illumination and turns to a uniform cyan blue under the UV light irradiation (365 nm). More images of the paper cranes under the UV light irradiation from different orientations are shown in the Figure S12 . Finally, the aqueous solution of F, N-GQDs is filled into an empty marker pen to write fluorescent characters on rice paper, which is invisible in daylight but shows a bright green fluorescence under UV light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 5 (f-g). There presented results demonstrate a significant potential of our synthesized F, N-GQDs in information security, anti-fake technique, and other related.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor film based on concentration effect of F, N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Flexible multi-color fluorescent films are prepared by mixing F, N-GQDs into a non-luminescent polymer (PVA). PVA (1 g) is added in deionized water (10 mL) under a stirring condition at 90 °C for 1 h to obtain PVA gel. In order to obtain multi-color fluorescent film, uniformly mixing the 5 mL F, N-GQDs solution with four different concentrations (such as 0.064 mg/mL, 0.2 mg/mL, 2 mg/mL and 5 mg/mL) with 5 mL PVA glue is required. Finally, drop-casting and natural dry of the F, N-GQDs/PVA gel on a glass slide form the flexible luminescent films. The obtained four different films determined by the concentration are named as film-1 (0.064 mg/mL), film-2 (0.2 mg/mL), film-3 (2 mg/mL) and film-4 (5 mg/mL), respectively. The corresponding production flow chart is shown in Fig. 6
(a). As the concentration of F, N-GQDs increases, the fluorescence of obtained films changes from blue to green, as the concentration of GQDs increases, as depicted in Fig. 6 (b). Notably, the corresponding transparency gradually decreases. More details can be seen in Figure S13 . In order to confirm the optical performance of films, fluorescence spectra and ultraviolet spectra of the four obtained films are explored. As shown in Fig. 6 (c) and Figure S14
(a-d), with the increase of F, N-GQDs concentration, the maximum emission peak shifts gradually from 442 to 501 nm under an optimal excitation wavelength, thus resulting in a fluorescence color change from blue to green. In addition, the obtained films also exhibit excitation dependent PL characteristics ( Figure S14
(e-h)), which are similar to the corresponding spectra of F, N-GQDs in solution, thus confirming that the PVA molecular play negligible effect on the optical properties of the embedded F, N-GQDs. Meanwhile, Fig. 6 (d) displays the UV–vis absorption spectra of the fluorescent films, the absorption peak exhibits an obvious red shift with the increase of the concentration. The bandgaps of the fluorescent films from film-1 to film-4 are calculated in Fig. 6 (e). It is shown that as the concentration increases, the energy gap gradually reduces, which agrees well with the red shift of PL emission. Notably, the fluorescence film has a good flexibility, as also demonstrated in Movie S1 . No folding trace appears in the flexible fluorescence film after being folded many times.

In conclusion, we developed a simple hydrothermal method to synthesize fluorine and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots, of which the emission wavelength can be tuned via modulating either the polarity or the concentration of solvents. Significantly, the fluorine atom played a vital role in tuning the emission wavelength. The solvatochromic phenomenon stemed from the interaction between F, N-GQDs and solvent molecules, while the concentration-dependent emission could be attributed to the hydrogen bonds within the self-aggregation. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of F, N-GQDs was strongly determined by the concentration of TC, and a F, N-GQDs-based probe for detecting TC was developed, with a detection limit of 77 nM in water. Besides, F, N-GQDs were further exploited as colorful fluorescent inks and flexible fluorescent film. Our synthesized F, N-GQDs, with tunable fluorescence in wavelength and intensity, have numerous opportunities for optical molecular sensors, information security, flexible optics, and others.

Changxing Wang: . Da Chen: Funding acquisition. Yongsheng Yang: . Siyuan Tang: Software. Xiameng Li: Software. Feng Xie: Formal analysis, Investigation. Gang Wang: Project administration. Qinglei Guo: .

C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2017A610104). The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ACS Appl. Mater. Interf.

RSC Adv.

Mater. Res. Bull.

Nanoscale

Nanoscale
